skill.name: planning-sprints
skill.description: Plan agile sprints by reviewing backlog items, estimating capacity, defining sprint goals, and producing structured sprint plans.
skill.summary: Guides sprint planning, backlog prioritization, capacity review, and sprint tracking for agile teams.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk matches, but the weak cryptography and reconnaissance hits are false positives from agile planning text, Markdown filenames, and example issue titles. The confirmed risk is legitimate but security-relevant use of Bash, GitHub CLI, local file writes, Python script examples, and task delegation, so publication is acceptable with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: External Command Workflow Requires User Review
medium_findings.description: The skill enables Bash and gives examples for gh CLI commands, cat, cp, and python3 scripts. These commands support sprint planning, but they can access project data, contact GitHub, and write sprint files, so users should review commands before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct command examples and the Bash tool declaration are present. The commands appear operational and legitimate, with no evidence of obfuscation or malicious endpoints.

low_findings.title: Agent Delegation Can Expand Operational Scope
low_findings.description: The skill recommends delegating GitHub operations, research, and plan validation to other agents. This is useful for sprint work, but delegated agents may perform additional actions outside the immediate planning text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The delegation instructions are explicit, but the file does not define the delegated agents or command payloads. This is a scope-control concern rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Crypto and Reconnaissance Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography and reconnaissance hits occur in ordinary planning text, Markdown paths, UI terms, issue examples, and API design references. No cryptographic implementation, credential handling, scanner, or network probing behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation and sprint-planning examples at the flagged locations. No security-sensitive algorithm calls or reconnaissance commands are present in those lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash and GitHub CLI Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains shell examples for listing issues and projects, reading templates, copying sprint plans, and invoking Python helper scripts. These patterns are expected for this workflow but should require normal command approval.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command snippets are explicit and repeated. They are not hidden payloads, but they do represent executable shell patterns.

content.value_statement: Sprint planning often fails when backlog scope, team capacity, and sprint goals are handled separately. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code produce structured sprint plans with clear priorities and realistic commitments.
actual_capabilities: Reviews backlog items and recommends sprint scope based on priority and capacity.
Applies RICE, MoSCoW, WSJF, and value-effort prioritization frameworks.
Calculates team capacity using sprint length, availability, holidays, and historical velocity.
Drafts sprint goals, success criteria, risks, dependencies, and definition of done.
Provides mid-sprint status checks, burndown interpretation, and scope adjustment recommendations.
Structures sprint reviews and retrospectives with action items for the next iteration.
limitations: It depends on accurate backlog, estimate, availability, and velocity data from the user or project tools.
Referenced helper scripts are described in the skill, but they are not present in this package.
GitHub CLI examples require local authentication and user approval before commands run.
It supports planning decisions, but it does not replace product owner or team commitment.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Sprint Plan
use_cases.description: Create a complete sprint plan with goals, capacity, selected backlog items, risks, and success criteria.

use_cases.title: Prioritize a Product Backlog
use_cases.description: Score candidate issues with structured prioritization methods before choosing work for the next sprint.

use_cases.title: Assess Sprint Health
use_cases.description: Review progress, remaining work, blockers, and scope options during an active sprint.

prompt_templates.title: Plan My Next Sprint
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner sprint planning with known backlog and team size.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan our next sprint. We have [team size] people, [sprint length] working days, and these candidate backlog items: [items]. Recommend a sprint goal, scope, and capacity target.

prompt_templates.title: Refine Backlog Priority
prompt_templates.prompt: Prioritize this backlog using RICE and MoSCoW. For each item, explain the score, priority class, dependencies, and whether it is ready for sprint planning: [backlog].

prompt_templates.title: Check Sprint Progress
prompt_templates.scenario: Mid-sprint review with metrics and blockers.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate our current sprint health. We committed [points], completed [points], have [days] days left, and these blockers: [blockers]. Recommend scope changes and next actions.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Full Sprint Operating Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced planning across goals, capacity, risk, and ceremonies.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a full sprint operating plan from this backlog, team calendar, velocity history, strategic objective, and risk list. Include sprint goal, selected work, capacity math, dependencies, ceremonies, definition of done, metrics, and retrospective focus.

output_examples.input: Plan Sprint 12 for a four-person team with ten working days and 35 average story points.
output_examples.output: A sprint plan with a focused goal, recommended 28 to 32 point commitment, selected high-priority items, risk notes, ceremony schedule, and success criteria.

output_examples.input: Prioritize our backlog before the next planning meeting.
output_examples.output: A ranked backlog grouped into must-have, should-have, and could-have work, with RICE reasoning and dependency warnings.

output_examples.input: We are halfway through the sprint and behind by five points.
output_examples.output: A sprint health summary with blocker analysis, descope options, stakeholder communication points, and immediate team actions.

best_practices: Provide current backlog items, estimates, team availability, and recent velocity before asking for a plan.
Review any suggested GitHub CLI or file-writing command before allowing it to run.
Use the output as a planning draft and confirm scope with the team before sprint start.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill with stale backlog data or missing acceptance criteria.
Do not commit to full theoretical capacity without buffer for meetings, support, and unknowns.
Do not let automated prioritization override product strategy or team judgment.

faq.question: Does this skill create sprint plans automatically?
faq.answer: It can draft detailed sprint plans, but users should review the scope, risks, and commands before acting on them.

faq.question: Can it work with GitHub issues?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes GitHub CLI examples for listing issues and projects when the local environment is authenticated.

faq.question: Does it include working helper scripts?
faq.answer: No. The skill references planning scripts, but this package only includes the main skill file and sprint plan template.

faq.question: Which prioritization methods are covered?
faq.answer: It covers RICE, MoSCoW, WSJF, and value-versus-effort prioritization.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it uses Bash, GitHub CLI, file writes, and delegated agents.

faq.question: Can it replace a Scrum master?
faq.answer: No. It supports facilitation and planning, but team discussion and product owner approval remain required.
